coredata.dc:title: Simple paper-based colorimetric and fluorescent glucose sensor using N-doped carbon dots and metal oxide hybrid structures
coredata.dc:creator: Ngo, Yen-Linh Thi
Nguyen, Phi Luan
Jana, Jayasmita
Choi, Won Mook
Chung, Jin Suk
Hur, Seung Hyun
coredata.dc:description: A new strategy for the fluorescent and colorimetric sensing of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose based on the metal oxide – carbon-dot hybrid structure was investigated. The sensing system is related to the catalytic oxidation reaction of glucose-by-glucose oxidase (GOx) to H2O2. In this study, a metal oxide hybrid with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (MFNCDs) that showed intrinsic peroxidase-like activity was synthesized and used as a catalyst instead of GOx to oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue-emitting oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The fluorescence of MFNCDs/TMB at 405 nm was quenched in the presence of H2O2 through the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer within MFNCDs, oxTMB, and glucose system. Therefore, the fluorescence and absorbance intensity can be applied to the quantitative determination of the concentration of H2O2 and glucose with a wide linear range. The detection limit for H2O2 and glucose based on the colorimetric method were as low as 84 nM and 0.41 μM, respectively. In contrast, the detection limit for H2O2 and glucose based on the fluorescent method were as low as 97 nM and 0.85 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the colorimetric readout on the paper device based on the changing color of the solution could also be integrated with a smartphone platform to conduct the on-site analysis of glucose without the use of the spectrometer. In addition, this dual sensor can be applied to detect glucose in real serum with highly accurate results, making it a good candidate for biosensor applications.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The morphology and size of NCDs, MFO, and MFNCDs were examined using SEM and TEM. The obtained NCDs had an agglomerated structure in the solid phase (Fig. 1A). Fig. 1B shows a TEM image of the NCDs, which possess a small spherical shape. The NCDs and were well dispersed in solution with a narrow size distribution of approximately 2.0–2.25 nm. The lattice spacing of as-prepared NCDs determined by HR-TEM was approximately 0.236 nm (Fig. 1C), which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [44], indicating the successful formation of a high-crystalline graphitic structure in the NCDs. The MFO exhibited a spherical shape, approximately 500 nm in size (Fig. S3). On the other hand, the MFNCDs composite was much smaller and the surface roughness was higher compared to that of pure metal oxides (Fig. 1D and E). The HR-TEM images (Figs. S3C and 1F) showed that both MFO and MFNCDs exhibited atomic lattice fringes of approximately 0.25, 0.271, 0.338, and 0.38 nm, which corresponds to the (110) and (104) plane of Fe2O3 [17,21], (110) plane of MnO2 [15], and (211) plane of Mn2O3 [45], respectively. The (100) plane of the graphitic structure was also acquired. Moreover, HR-TEM also further confirmed the decoration of NCDs on the metal oxides structure, showing that the NCDs served as a support and were beneficial to their formation as well as for reducing the size of the metal oxides.This study was supported by the Basic Science Research Program through the National Research Foundation of Korea (NRF) funded by The Ministry of Science, ICT and Future Planning (2019R1A2B5B02069683).

head.ce:title: Simple paper-based colorimetric and fluorescent glucose sensor using N-doped carbon dots and metal oxide hybrid structures

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A new strategy for the fluorescent and colorimetric sensing of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) and glucose based on the metal oxide – carbon-dot hybrid structure was investigated. The sensing system is related to the catalytic oxidation reaction of glucose-by-glucose oxidase (GOx) to H2O2. In this study, a metal oxide hybrid with nitrogen-doped carbon dots (MFNCDs) that showed intrinsic peroxidase-like activity was synthesized and used as a catalyst instead of GOx to oxidize 3,3′,5,5′-tetramethylbenzidine (TMB) to blue-emitting oxidized TMB (oxTMB) in the presence of hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). The fluorescence of MFNCDs/TMB at 405 nm was quenched in the presence of H2O2 through the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer within MFNCDs, oxTMB, and glucose system. Therefore, the fluorescence and absorbance intensity can be applied to the quantitative determination of the concentration of H2O2 and glucose with a wide linear range. The detection limit for H2O2 and glucose based on the colorimetric method were as low as 84 nM and 0.41 μM, respectively. In contrast, the detection limit for H2O2 and glucose based on the fluorescent method were as low as 97 nM and 0.85 μM, respectively. Furthermore, the colorimetric readout on the paper device based on the changing color of the solution could also be integrated with a smartphone platform to conduct the on-site analysis of glucose without the use of the spectrometer. In addition, this dual sensor can be applied to detect glucose in real serum with highly accurate results, making it a good candidate for biosensor applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Diabetes mellitus is one of a group of metabolic disorders related to glucose, which can cause multi-system damage, such as eyes, kidneys, heart, and other human organs [1]. Hence, the early detection and regular monitoring of the glucose levels in the blood are key factors in preventing the complications of diabetes. In recent years, enormous efforts have been made to determine the glucose concentration with high accuracy, such as surface-enhanced Raman scattering, electrochemical, chemiluminescence, fluorescence, and colorimetric approaches [2–7]. Among these approaches, colorimetric and fluorescent methods have attracted tremendous attention owing to their simplicity, low-experimental cost, high sensitivity, portability, and recognition of color discrimination. On the other hand, many studies have been based on single signal determination (colorimetric or fluorescence). On the other hand, the single signal output could be affected by external conditions, such as operation and instrument efficiency [8,9]. Moreover, there are few colorimetric and fluorescent dual-signal sensor for glucose with an expected rapid response and feasible measurement available. Therefore, a method with dual signals to detect glucose is needed.

In a typical colorimetric method, glucose would be oxidized by glucose oxidase (GOx) to generate gluconolactone and H2O2. The concentration of glucose is then measured by assessing the change from a colorless to a colored product of the peroxidase substrate in the presence of a peroxidase catalyst [10]. Nevertheless, the use of natural enzymes as a peroxidase catalyst still has some drawbacks, including adversely affected by environmental factors and the relatively high cost. To solve this problem, it is essential to investigate proper peroxidase mimetic nanomaterials to substitute for natural enzymes. Several studies have reported that various transition metal oxides nanoparticles (NPs) [11], metal particles [10], perovskite NPs [12], and metal oxides nanocomposite with carbon materials [13,14] can catalyze the reaction through the peroxidase-catalytic activity. Recently, ferric oxide and manganese oxide have received a great interest across technological applications, including energy storage [15,16], catalyst [17], magnetism [18,19], environmental [20], sensor [21,22], drug delivery [23], owing to their outstanding structural combined with chemical and physical properties. Apart from unique properties, ferric oxide and manganese oxide have been reported to show excellent peroxidase-like activity. The use of composites of both as the enzyme peroxidase material has not been reported widely. Accordingly, a well-designed morphology and enhanced active site of the catalytic material are desired.

Among the ideal nanomaterials, carbon dots (CDs) are potential candidates for chemical sensing and bioimaging applications owing to their low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility, superior photostability, eco-friendly, tunable fluorescence, and distinct optical properties. The introduction of an N atom to the graphitic carbon structures can enhance their electrical property and chemical stability, which leads to improved working performance towards specific target materials. Recently, CDs as well as N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) have been utilized for a range of applications, such as oxygen reduction reaction [24,25], water splitting [26,27], biosensors [28,29], batteries [30], bioimaging [31], and photocatalysts [32]. A versatile method to fabricate novel NCDs fluorescent materials used the hydrothermal process of different saccharides [33,34]. After the hydrothermal process, saccharides form carbonaceous nanomaterials, which can be functionalized further to improve the optical and fluorescent properties of the obtained carbon dots. Moreover, to enrich the potential applications, decorating other metal oxides with NCDs is an efficient way of tuning the unique electronic, catalytic, and optical properties. Thus, the introduction of NCDs to metal oxides will enhance their versatile properties further.

Along with the development of information technology, the smartphone has become more easily accessible. Recently, some proposed methods using smartphones as detectors for advanced detection applications such as colorimetric, fluorescence and electrochemiluminescence sensing were developed [35–40]. This approach not only provides portable, rapid response, cost-effective diagnostic equipment but also enables on-demand analysis with reliable results. Therefore, the development of an integrated visual testing platform based on a combination of smartphone and composite material has become a great potential alternative to traditional diagnostic testing devices in analytical applications.

In this research, uric acid was used as a direct monomer source for the fabricated of NCDs along with glucose using a bottom-up method. Owing to the interaction between the aldehyde groups of glucose and the amine groups of uric acid without the assistance of a catalyst [41], it was expected that the product of the reaction would lead to N-containing carbon nanostructures. The composite with NCDs decorated on manganese oxide/ferric oxide (MFNCDs), as a new peroxidase mimetic catalyst, was fabricated using a simple one-pot hydrothermal process. The results showed that the NCDs were well dispersed over the metal oxide surface, which provided more beneficial interface active sites and enhanced the intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity towards TMB in the presence of H2O2 to generate the blue color product (oxTMB). Moreover, the fluorescence of the MFNCDs/TMB system can be quenched with the presence of H2O2 owing to the inner filter effect (IFE) and electron transfer between the components in the system. Hence, with the aforementioned properties, a dual sensor based on colorimetric and fluorescent method for the quantitative determination of the concentration of H2O2 and glucose was developed. The additional advantages, including color readout on the paper-sensing device via an application (APP) integrated with a smartphone, low cytotoxicity, good biocompatibility as well as feasibility in the real samples, were outstanding characteristics for practical applications.
Detailed information on the materials and analysis apparatus is described in the supporting materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of MnxOy/Fe2O3/N-doped carbon dots composite (MFNCDs)
ce:section.ce:para: The MFNCDs composite was prepared using a one-pot hydrothermal process according to a previous study with minor modifications [6]. In a typical method, CH3COONa (0.18 g) was dissolved in 20 mL ethylene glycol (EG). After stirring for 10 min, 20 mg of MnCl2·4H2O and 82 mg of FeCl3·6H2O were added to the mixture solution with vigorously stirring until it became a homogeneous solution. The colorless solution turned gradually orange, which was denoted as solution 1. In another method, a pre-calculated amount of glucose and 17 mg of uric acid were dispersed in 20 mL DI water, and ultrasonicated for 20 min to make an aqueous solution 2. Subsequently, solution 2 was added to the mixture solution 1 and transferred to a Teflon-stainless steel autoclave. The autoclave was then heated at 180 °C for 12 h and cooled naturally to room temperature. The obtained brown solution was centrifuged at 9000 rpm to eliminate the large particles. The solution was purified further with DI water using dialysis tubing with a pore size of 1000 Da for 6 h to remove all the impurities. For comparison of the peroxidase-like catalytic activity, metal oxides mixture (MFO) and N-doped carbon dots (NCDs) were obtained by placing solutions 1 and 2 separately in the autoclave. Moreover, MnxOy/NCDs (MNCDs) and Fe2O3/NCDs (FNCDs) were also synthesized using the same method in the absence of precursor Fe and Mn, respectively. The conditions of MFCNDs synthesis were studied and optimized using a MATLAB simulation, as presented in Fig. S1. Scheme 1 presents the fabrication process of the MFNCDs composite.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescence for the determination of H2O2 and glucose
ce:section.ce:para: For H2O2 determination, a series of H2O2 solutions with different concentrations (50 μL), 10 μL of MFNCDs solution (5 mg mL−1), and 60 μL of TMB were added to a NaAc-HAc buffer (0.1 M, pH = 3.6). After reacting for a few minutes at room temperature, the absorption and fluorescence spectra were recorded separately. The spectra were collected at 652 nm with an excitation wavelength of 315 nm using a UV–Vis spectrometer and fluorescence spectrophotometer at each H2O2 concentration. Moreover, to evaluate the stabilization of color development for accurate measurements, H2SO4 (1 M) was added to the mixture as a stop solution, and the UV–Vis measurements were repeated, as described above. The data were collected at 450 nm. The fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 275 nm.

For glucose determination, the target concentration of glucose and 50 μL of GOx (1 mg mL−1) were added to phosphate buffer saline (PBS, 0.01 M, 250 μL, pH = 7). After incubation at 37 °C for 30 min, 10 μL of MFNCDs (5.0 mg mL−1) and 60 μL of TMB were added successively to the above mixture, and the final volume reached 950 μL with 0.1 M NaAc-HAc buffer (pH = 3.6). After a reaction for some time without further incubation, the absorbance at 652 nm and the fluorescence spectra (λex = 315 nm) were obtained at each glucose concentration. Similar to the H2O2 detection part, H2SO4 was also added to the solution, and the experiments were carried out in a similar manner.

The stability of the sensor based on MFNCDs was examined in a similar manner as introduced above within a month under optimal conditions. The selectivity of MFCNDs towards the interference species was evaluated by conducting selectivity experiments in a similar manner to that mentioned above, except that glucose was replaced with different interfering reagents. The interfering reagent concentration was five times higher than that of glucose to observe the effects clearly. In addition, to assess the practicability of MFCNDs in real samples, the experiments were conducted using real sera, such as human serum, serum (HS), and rabbit serum (RS). The HS and RS were used as received and diluted at various ratios, and glucose was added into the human serum samples using the standard addition method. The human serum samples were obtained by centrifugation at 3000 rpm for 10 min. The results were compared with the results determined using a commercial glucometer.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of glucose via data processing by smartphone
ce:section.ce:para: The paper analysis devices after reaction (PAD) were placed into a studio box and illuminated with a daylight lamp. The smartphone was placed on a tripod and fixed at a distance of 10 cm from the PAD. The images were cropped and transferred to the library. The Red (R), Green (G), and Blue (B) values were analyzed using a self-developed color detector application (APP) on a smartphone. The glucose concentration was then measured via the APP based on the relationship between the R, G, and B parameters and concentration; the glucose levels were obtained immediately.

Scheme 1 presents the approach for fabricating the MFCNDs. The crystallographic phase of the as-obtained samples was characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD). As shown in Fig. S2, the XRD pattern of NCDs showed a broad peak at approximately 25.3° 2θ, which corresponded to graphitic-like carbon systems (002) (JCPDS 08–0415) [34,42]. The interlayer spacing of d 002 = 0.38 nm was higher than that of the ideal graphite due to the incorporation of heteroatoms on the basal plane, which was disordered in the graphitic structure [33,43]. The XRD pattern of MFO showed the main characteristic peaks of Fe2O3 (JCPDS 33–0664), Mn2O3 (JCPDS 41–1442), and MnO2 (JCPDS 42–1316). After the hydrothermal reaction, all the XRD peaks of NCDs and MFO were observed on the composite XRD pattern, which indicates the successful formation of the nanocomposites. No other impurities existed in the composite because no other peaks except for the peaks of NCDs and MFO were observed. On the other hand, the peak for the crystal plane (002) was broadened, which suggests that the obtained NCDs and MFNCDs exhibit a small particle size. These results were consistent with those of X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM) results.

The morphology and size of NCDs, MFO, and MFNCDs were examined using SEM and TEM. The obtained NCDs had an agglomerated structure in the solid phase (Fig. 1 A). Fig. 1B shows a TEM image of the NCDs, which possess a small spherical shape. The NCDs and were well dispersed in solution with a narrow size distribution of approximately 2.0–2.25 nm. The lattice spacing of as-prepared NCDs determined by HR-TEM was approximately 0.236 nm (Fig. 1C), which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphitic carbon [44], indicating the successful formation of a high-crystalline graphitic structure in the NCDs. The MFO exhibited a spherical shape, approximately 500 nm in size (Fig. S3). On the other hand, the MFNCDs composite was much smaller and the surface roughness was higher compared to that of pure metal oxides (Fig. 1D and E). The HR-TEM images (Figs. S3C and 1F) showed that both MFO and MFNCDs exhibited atomic lattice fringes of approximately 0.25, 0.271, 0.338, and 0.38 nm, which corresponds to the (110) and (104) plane of Fe2O3 [17,21], (110) plane of MnO2 [15], and (211) plane of Mn2O3 [45], respectively. The (100) plane of the graphitic structure was also acquired. Moreover, HR-TEM also further confirmed the decoration of NCDs on the metal oxides structure, showing that the NCDs served as a support and were beneficial to their formation as well as for reducing the size of the metal oxides.

Further evidence for the formation of MFNCDs was obtained by comparing the XPS spectra of MFNCDs with those of the samples (Fig. S4). In the XPS survey spectra of MFNCDs, five main peaks at binding energies of 286.11, 400.28, 532.25, 641.86, and 711.66 eV corresponded to C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Mn 2p, and Fe 2p, respectively (Fig. S4A). The N atomic percentage in the MFNCDs was approximately 8.81%, confirming the formation of N-doped carbon structures. Tables S2–S6 present the detailed relative ratio of C 1s, N 1s, O 1s, Mn 2p, and Fe 2p bonding states. Detailed characterizations using other techniques like high resolution spectra each elements by XPS (Fig. S4), FT-IR (Fig. S5), and Tyndal effect (Fig. S6) are furnished in the supporting information.

The optical properties of NCDs and MFNCDs were investigated by ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectroscopy and photoluminescence (PL) spectroscopy (Fig. S7). The spectra of the NCDs and MFNCDs showed strong absorption peaks at 290 and 260 nm, respectively, which were assigned to π−π∗ electronic transitions of the aromatic sp2 domains [46,47]. The UV–Vis spectrum also displayed another absorption band in the region, 320–325 nm, for both NCDs and MFNCDs, corresponding to the n−π∗ electronic transitions of between C and O or C and N double bonds (CO/CN) in the graphitic carbon structure [48]. The PL emission spectra were analyzed at various excitation wavelengths in both samples. The PL emission spectra showed peaks at 468 and 415 nm for NCDs and MFNCDs, respectively, under excitation of 330 nm owing to the transition between the lone pair of electrons in the valence band and the δ∗ conduction band, which was attributed to the sp2 C–N band [5,48]. The image inset in Fig. S7C shows that the MFNCDs solution is slightly brown under visible light, whereas it exhibits bright blue fluorescence under UV light (λ = 365 nm). The PL emission spectra of NCDs and MFNCDs were almost independent of the excitation wavelength. Moreover, no noticeable change in the emission peak position was observed, indicating that the as-synthesized MFNCDs had a single emitter with the primary emission color being blue.

The relative quantum yield (QY) was also calculated to evaluate the fluorescence properties of MFNCDs using quinine sulfate as a standard reference (QY 54%) under an excitation wavelength of 330 nm. The calculated QY of MFNCDs was 37.9%, which is lower than that of pure NCDs (40.0%). This might have been due to the presence of metal atoms on the NCDs surface (Fig. S8). The time-correlated single-photon counting (TCSPC) showed an exponential decay profile of NCDs and MFNCDs composite, as shown in Fig. S9. The average lifetime of MFNCDs (7.52 ns) was much shorter than that of the NCDs (10.29 ns). Table S7 lists the corresponding fractional contributions. The profile shows that the slowest component decreased from 82.95% to 28.37%, and the faster components increased from 17.05% to 62.08%. In particular, the new fastest component appeared in the MFNCDs composite, which would cause the short lifetime of the MFNCDs. Furthermore, the long lifetime of NCDs might be governed by electron-hole trapping occurring at different functional groups on the NCDs surface. Hence, it is likely to be influenced by the interaction with the metal counterparts, which causes a decrease in the lifetime of MFNCDs [49].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Intrinsic peroxidase-like catalytic activity of MFNCDs
ce:section.ce:para: A set of experiments for catalytic oxidations taking TMB as a colorimetric substrate were performed to determine if the as-synthesized MFNCDs exhibit peroxidase activity. As depicted in Fig. 2 , MFNCDs catalyze the TMB substrate to change from colorless to blue in the presence of H2O2 with a distinct absorption peak at 652 nm. Concurrently, there were no obvious absorption peaks obtained in the control experiments, such as those of the TMB in the presence of either H2O2 or MFNCDs catalyst alone, showing that the reaction must proceed with both components. The enzyme-mimetic peroxidase-like activity of MFNCDs is much greater than those of pure NCDs, MFO, MNCDs, and FNCDs under similar conditions (Fig. S10); indicating the synergistic effect between metal oxides and carbon dots. As shown in Fig. S7(A-C), a bathochromic shift (red shift) occurs for the MFNCDs when compared to pure NCDs, which is consistent with a suggested mechanism that electron transfer can occur from the NCDs to metal oxides [50]. Moreover, in this sensing system, TMB molecules are absorbed on the surface of the composite and donate lone-pair electron in the amino groups to MFNCDs composite, which confers an increased electron density and mobility of composite. Furthermore, the charge transfer n-type of the metal oxides increases the Fermi level [51], and also the involvement of the high electrical conductivity of NCDs [52], which helped mediate electron transfer from composite to H2O2, thus, leading to its faster breakdown into hydroxyl radicals (•OH) and H2O by MFNCDs catalyst composite. These results proved that the as-prepared MFNCDs have excellent peroxidase mimetic activity that can accelerate the oxidation of TMB.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optimization the reaction conditions for the oxidation of TMB
ce:section.ce:para: To determine the optimal catalytic conditions for the oxidation of TMB in the presence of MFNCDs as a catalyst, the effects of pH, the amount of MFNCDs, TMB concentration, and reaction time were investigated (Fig. S11). Accordingly, the optimal experimental conditions to acquire the maximum catalytic activity of MFNCDs are as follows: pH = 3.6, 10 mM of TMB, and 30 min for incubation. All the experiments were conducted under the optimal conditions.

To explain the catalytic mechanism further and compare these catalysts, the parameters of steady-state kinetics for the peroxidase-like activity were determined by varying the concentration of one substrate (TMB or H2O2) while keeping the other unchanged. The Michaelis-Menten constant (Km) and maximum initial velocity (Vmax) were acquired using the Lineweaver-Burk plot of the double reciprocal of the Michaelis-Menten equation and are shown in Table S8. A higher Vmax suggests high catalytic activity, whereas a smaller Km value reflects a higher affinity between the enzymes and substrates [53]. As summarized in Table S8, the Km value for MFNCDs with TMB as the substrate was approximately 6.3, 2.3, and 31.9 times lower than those of NCDs, MFOs, and horseradish peroxidase – natural enzyme (HRP), respectively, showing that the MFNCDs possessed stronger affinity towards TMB. Furthermore, the Km value of MFNCDs with H2O2 as the substrate was significantly lower than those of the others, which is one of the lowest values ever reported (Table S8), suggesting that a very low concentration of H2O2 is required to obtain the maximal catalytic activity for MFNCDs. These results show that the as-synthesized MFNCDs have high affinity for both TMB and the H2O2 substrate.

In addition, the catalytic activity of the MFNCDs with different ranges of TMB and H2O2 concentrations was measured. Fig. S12(G-H) presents double reciprocal plots of the initial velocity vs. concentration of a single substrate, which was acquired for a range of concentration of the other substrate. The parallel lines in the plots presented a ping-pong mechanism, as was also observed for HRP and other materials [50]. This suggests that, like HRP, the MFNCDs can bind and react with the first substrate throughout the reaction, and then release the first product before reacting with the second substrate.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Colorimetric and fluorescent response mechanism for H2O2 and glucose detection
ce:section.ce:para: As demonstrated above, MFNCDs have properties similar to HRP owing to the presence of the ferric component in the structure. Hence, they can oxidize TMB in the presence of H2O2. The redox cycle of MFNCDs consists of two steps. The first step occurred through the two-electron oxidation of MFNCDs that was initiated by oxidizing the substrate H2O2, while the second step that occurred via the two-electron reduction of MFNCDs by TMB substrate [54]. After completing two successive processes, the colorless TMB was converted to a blue-colored product (oxidized TMB - oxTMB) with a single-electron oxidation state.

The UV–Vis spectra were used to identify the different component entities of TMB, as shown in Fig. S13A. As reported in previous studies [55,56], the reaction components of TMB include the initial TMB state (diamine form) with an absorbance max at ∼285 nm, followed by the charge transfer complex (CTC) with an absorbance max at 652 nm, and the complete oxidation state of the diamine form (two-electron loss) with an absorbance max at 450 nm. This study showed that the diamine TMB form (black line) could be oxidized to form two intermediate oxidation-state products, including a colorless TMB radical cation and CTC as a blue-colored intermediate oxidation state (red line), when MFNCDs facilitated one-electron oxidation. Moreover, CTC consists of a colorless diamine form (electron donor) and yellow-colored diamine form (electron acceptor) (Scheme S1). After the second one-electron oxidation occurs, the blue-colored TMB is converted to its yellow diamine oxidation product (green line). Therefore, the CTC can be observed at 652 nm (blue-colored – red line), while the diamine oxidation state can be observed at 450 nm (yellow-colored – green line), as mentioned above. Based on the oxidation state of TMB, these phenomena were used to determine both H2O2 and glucose with the pre-acid stopped blue-colored and acid-stopped yellow-colored, respectively.

The MFNCDs exhibited emission at 415 nm under excitation at 330 nm (Fig. S12B). When the MFNCDs were mixed with an oxTMB solution (red line in Fig. S12B), the PL emission at 405 nm showed a slight change. Moreover, as shown in Fig. S12C, the absorption spectra of oxTMB showed a sufficient overlap region with the excitation and emission spectra of MFNCDs. Thus, fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) and inner filter effect (IFE) can occur [57–59]. FRET is involved with the spectral overlap between the emission spectrum of the fluorescent probe and the absorption spectrum of the acceptor. Whereas IFE refers to the absorption of the quencher overlapped with excitation (primary IFE) or emission (secondary IFE) of the carbon dots. Thus, the mechanism of quenching could be confusing in some cases. However, the distinction between quenching mechanism can be further clarified through decrease in lifetime and change of UV–Vis spectra. As shown in Fig. S12D, the UV–Vis spectra of oxTMB with and without of MFCNDs were similar, which indicated that the ground-state complex was not formed [14]. Again, as presented in Table S7, the fluorescence decay profile showed no significant change in the average lifetime of the MFNCDs system even after adding the high concentration of analytes (5 mM) [58], which indicated that the FRET mechanism can be excluded. On the other hand, the zeta potential of MFNCDs/oxTMB and MFNCDs/oxTMB/Glu system were measured to be −4.08 and −8.10 mV, respectively, which can be attributed to the formation •OH radical groups on the surface. In addition, the lower average lifetime of the MFNCDs/TMB/Glu system (6.45 ns) compared to pristine MFNCDs (7.52 ns) can be explained by the modification of recombination processes within the MFNCDs system [60]. This result is consistent with previous reports of a decrease in the lifetime of MFNCDs particles in the presence of external agents [61]. Furthermore, the glucose sensing process includes the production of peroxide that induces a decrease in PL intensity of the probe through electron transfer followed by fluorescence quenching in the system [62]. Therefore, the fluorescence quenching corresponded to IFE and electron transfer within MFNCDs, oxTMB, and glucose system. Therefore, the absorbance of MFNCDs/oxTMB at 652 nm (pre-acid stopped) and 450 nm (acid stopped), and the PL intensity of MFNCDs/oxTMB were used for the quantitative colorimetric and fluorescence sensing of H2O2 and glucose.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 and glucose concentration using the MFNCDs-based colorimetric sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the excellent peroxidase-like activity of MFNCDs, a colorimetric method to detect H2O2 and glucose using MFNCDs as a catalyst through a color reaction was carried out. Because the absorbance of oxTMB is in proportion to the concentration of H2O2, it is a facile approach to determine H2O2 at 652 nm (pre-acid stopped) and 450 nm (acid stopped) using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. As shown in Fig. 3 (A–B), two broad linear relationships between the relative absorbance and H2O2 concentration in the ranges of 100 nM to 2 mM and 2 mM–10 mM were obtained with a detection limit (LOD) of 84 nM (S/N = 3), which is much lower than the detection limit of catalyst-based magnetic nanoparticles or quantum dot composites. Furthermore, after stopping the reaction with sulfuric acid, the absorbance spectra of oxTMB at 450 nm also showed a positive curvature to the concentration of H2O2 within a similar range of pre-acid stopped (Fig. S14(A-B)). The LOD for H2O2 (acid stopped) was estimated to be 76 nM (S/N = 3). Moreover, the color variation and the absorbance-response curve of H2O2 was easily recognizable by a visual observation under the optimal conditions, even lower than 100 nM, as shown in Fig. S15.

Glucose oxidase (GOx) was used to catalyze the oxidation of glucose to generate gluconolactone and H2O2, in which as-produced H2O2 is the main product of the reaction. MFNCDs catalyzed the as-obtained H2O2 and then reacted with TMB, leading to a color change caused by the conversion of TMB to oxTMB. Hence, the colorimetric determination of glucose can be performed using MFNCDs as an artificial enzyme. Owing to the easy degeneration in an acidic environment, the glucose detection process could be proceeded via two steps, as mentioned in the experimental part. Fig. 3(C-D) shows the absorbance at 652 nm, which increased with increasing glucose concentration from 500 nM to 10 mM. The two linear ranges for glucose were 500 nM to 1 mM and 1 mM–10 mM with LODs as low as 0.41 μM (S/N = 3). Similar to the H2O2 measurement, the oxidation reaction could be stopped by adding acid. Two linear ranges, similar to pre-acid stopped methods and LOD, were calculated to be 0.72 μM (S/N = 3) (Fig. S14(C-D)). As shown in Fig. S16, the color variation became perceivable by the naked eye at the same volume with the lowest glucose concentration of 500 nM, which is very convenient for detection. Table S9 lists the sensing performance of the H2O2 and glucose detection system, along with some previously reported systems.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of H2O2 and glucose concentrations using the MFNCDs-based fluorescent sensor
ce:section.ce:para: A series of H2O2 and glucose solutions with different concentrations also could be detected by the fluorescent sensor. Based on IFE and electron transfer in the system, Fig. 4 depicts the PL intensity of MFNCDs/oxTMB decreased gradually with increasing H2O2 and glucose concentrations, respectively. Stern-Volmer plots of the relative PL intensity vs. the H2O2 and glucose concentrations were linear, indicating a strong dependence on the concentrations. Two linear ranges in a wide region, 0 nM to 15 mM, were obtained with excellent correlation coefficients. The LOD for H2O2 and glucose detection were 97 nM and 0.85 μM (S/N = 3), respectively. Furthermore, as shown in Fig. S17, the determination of H2O2 and glucose also could be measured, even after stopping the reaction by acid. The PL response was linearly correlated with the H2O2 and glucose concentrations in two wide ranges: 500 nM–15 mM with a low LOD of 51 nM for H2O2 and 0.9 μM for glucose (S/N = 3). Moreover, compared to the other reported values, this fluorescence method based on MFNCDs showed a lower detection limit and a much wider linear range, as shown in Table S10. These data further demonstrated that this method is beneficial for clinical and medical applications with a wide linear range.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Study on inference during the diagnosis of diabetes and the stability of biosensors
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the MFNCDs/TMB/GOx sensing system was evaluated using this dual signal sensor with the fluorescence and UV–Vis absorbance response in the presence of other common interfering species. As shown in Fig. 5 , both colorimetric and fluorescence sensing systems showed no significant response to ascorbic acid, uric acid, l-cysteine, dopamine, glycine, sorbitol, glutathione, and other saccharides, even if the concentration was up to 5 times that of glucose. Moreover, Fig. S18 shows that at a 1:1 ratio of other interference species and glucose, there was no obvious change in the response of the system. These results showed that the sensor-based MFNCDs exhibit excellent selectivity for glucose detection.

Furthermore, the stability of MFNCDs/TMB/GOx-based sensor was also investigated via using dual-signal equipment. As presented in Fig. S19, the relative absorbance and PL intensity of the sensing system were shown no significant change under optimized conditions after 30 days. Hence, this implies that MFNCDs possessed excellent stability for biosensor applications.

To validate the applicability of MFNCDs/TMB/GOx for the detection of glucose, the method was applied to measure the glucose concentration in real samples. Because the sample was collected from a volunteer, the human blood needs centrifuging to remove the heavy particles. The samples were diluted 100 times and gave a high significant % recovery (Table S11). The experiments were then performed using rabbit serum (RS), horse serum (HS), and human serum, which were known as the concentration of glucose under optimized conditions. Each concentration obtained was compared with those acquired by the commercial glucometer. These results are listed in Table 1 for human blood and Table S12 – S13 for RS and HS, respectively. The differences in the obtained concentration between the fluorescent sensor based on MFNCDs/TMB/GOx and the commercial glucometer varied from 1.49 to 3.03%. These results are consistent with those obtained by the colorimetric method, with a low percentage difference from 0.97 to 2.35%. The determination of glucose by the fluorescent and colorimetric methods showed negligible differences compared to the commercial glucometer, confirming that the as-fabricated MFNCDs can detect glucose precisely, even in real samples.

For biomedical applications, the biocompatibility of MFNCDs in the cellular environment was discussed. The cytotoxicity of MFNCDs was studied using a standard MTT assay with HT-1080 cells. No significant decrease in cell viability (94.8%) was observed for cells incubated with MFNCDs, as displayed in Fig. S20, even at ultra-high concentrations of up to 805 μg/mL for 24 h. Furthermore, to examine the potential of MFNCDs for cellular imaging, HT-1080 cells were incubated with MFNCDs for 8 h, followed by confocal fluorescence microscopy imaging. As shown in the confocal microscopy images (Fig. S21), the HT-1080 cells emitted bright blue fluorescence under excitation at 405 nm owing to the excitation-dependent fluorescence properties of MFNCDs. In addition, the fluorescence emission of MFNCDs was observed predominantly in the cytoplasmic area of cells, showing that the MFNCDs penetrated the membranes of HT-1080 cells and diffused into the cytoplasm, but did not enter the inner nuclei. Therefore, these results show that the MFNCDs is a promising sensing and imaging material for in vivo bio-imaging applications with excellent biocompatibility.

The UV–Vis spectrophotometer is typically required for almost colorimetric methods using a complicated operating process. Hence, it is necessary to develop a rapid, portable, and convenient on-site analysis for practical implementation. Moreover, the color change of the solution with the various glucose concentrations can be realized by the naked eye, as shown in Fig. S16. In this study, the colorimetric sensing system integrated with a smartphone was developed using an artificial neural network-based APP, as designed in Fig. S22.

The APP for analyzing the glucose level is a software for estimating the concentration of glucose through image processing (Scheme S2). The principle of this software is based on modeling the relationship between the glucose concentrations and the colors of the PAD. The data from experience are collected to train the network. The model can then be used to calculate the unknown data. First, the PAD was taken or collected by users. After the reaction with glucose, the white PAD (control pad) and blue PAD/yellow PAD (detection pad) were chosen as a background and detection region, respectively. The software could then analyze the PAD and output of the region of interest (ROI) values. The three-color channels of the ROI, which are R, G, and B, were split, and the intensity rates were calculated. In addition, the background subtraction technique was applied to reduce the effects of the lighting environment.

Owing to the complications of finding a function based on three variables (intensity rate of R, G, B) for the analysis data, the artificial neural network is preferred, but there are some advantages, including generalization, model non-linear, and simple relationships. Consequently, the network can suggest unknown relationships on unknown data after training from well-known initial inputs, which allows a prediction of an unknown concentration of glucose. Three neural networks for modeling the relationship between the blue color of PAD (pre-acid stopped), yellow color of PAD (acid stopped), and both (blue and yellow) with the glucose concentration are proposed in this work. For only color detection (blue or yellow), the neural network configuration contained three or six input data (intensity rate of R, G, B), one hidden layer (20 or 30 neurons), and one output (concentration of glucose). The dataset for training and testing used 83% and 17%, respectively. Fig. 6 shows the results of glucose analysis using a smartphone platform, which is consistent with those results using a glucometer (Table S14). In particular, the users can use either blue or yellow, or both (blue and yellow) for glucose detection with no noticeable error, but the results of using both data showed reasonable accuracy and reliability. Therefore, the MFNCDs and the method proposed in this study can be applied to detect glucose without the need for a spectrophotometer.

The dual signal sensor for H2O2 and glucose were developed successfully using fluorescent and colorimetric methods based on the oxidation of TMB in the presence of MFNCDs as a peroxidase mimetic catalyst. The sensing system delivered a wide detection range and a low detection limit for the detection of both H2O2 and glucose. The obtained MFNCDs-based sensor exhibited high selectivity against various common interfering species, long-term stability, excellent bio-imaging properties, and low cytotoxicity for bio-medical applications. Moreover, this dual sensor also recognized the glucose levels in real serum samples, indicating the practicality of glucose detection using a fluorescent and colorimetric sensing system. Furthermore, a portable, facile, high accuracy, rapid response, easy-to-use, and low-cost paper sensor device based on a colorimetric method integrated with a smartphone platform was designed for the detection of glucose in clinical diagnostics and food industry applications.
